Name: curvature_aware_precision_switching
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Switching: Training-Free Spectral Routing for Layer-Wise Quantization Stability
Short Hypothesis: Layer-wise precision decisions in quantized neural networks can be determined pre-inference via deterministic spectral diagnostics of activation Hessians and gradient covariance, without requiring training or calibration. Specifically, we hypothesize that blocks exhibiting high curvature concentration (quantified via condition numbers and spectral decay rates of empirical Hessian estimates) require higher precision to maintain PAC-Bayes generalization bounds, while low-curvature blocks tolerate aggressive quantization. This setting is ideal because: (1) current mixed-precision strategies rely on post-hoc calibration or expensive search, (2) spectral geometry provides mathematically rigorous stability certificates unavailable through heuristic approaches, and (3) training-free routing enables per-input adaptive precision without retraining, a capability not systematically explored in prior work.
Related Work: Existing mixed-precision quantization methods (e.g., BRECQ, ZipML) employ layer-wise or channel-wise precision selection via calibration data and post-training optimization, requiring expensive search over precision configurations. Recent work on Hessian-based pruning (e.g., movement pruning, fisher-mask) uses second-order information but focuses on sparsity, not precision. Training-free quantization approaches (e.g., OCS, ZeroQ) estimate optimal scales without training but do not exploit curvature geometry for precision routing. Concurrent work on neural network geometry (e.g., lottery ticket hypothesis, mode connectivity) analyzes loss landscape structure but does not connect it to quantization precision decisions. Our contribution is novel because: (1) we formalize the connection between local Hessian conditioning and precision requirements via explicit PAC-Bayes bounds, (2) we propose a deterministic, pre-execution diagnostic loop that requires no calibration data or training, (3) we provide formal stability radii guaranteeing that quantized blocks remain within bounded perturbation regimes of their full-precision counterparts, and (4) we explicitly quantify amortized diagnostic overhead and prove it vanishes relative to inference cost.
Abstract: Mixed-precision quantization reduces inference cost but requires expensive precision selection via calibration or search. We propose a training-free, curvature-aware precision routing framework that determines layer-wise quantization levels deterministically from pre-execution spectral diagnostics. Our key insight is that blocks exhibiting high Hessian condition numbers and steep spectral decay (indicating sharp loss landscape features) require higher precision to preserve generalization, while low-curvature blocks tolerate aggressive quantization. We develop three core contributions: (1) a formal PAC-Bayes analysis connecting local curvature to precision-dependent generalization bounds, establishing that condition number κ and target error ε imply minimum precision p(κ, ε) via explicit bit-width formulas; (2) a deterministic, O(1)-overhead diagnostic protocol using Hutchinson trace estimation and Lanczos eigenvalue decomposition to compute per-block curvature proxies without training; (3) a block-level precision assignment algorithm that routes each layer to {FP32, FP16, INT8} based on spectral thresholds calibrated to maintain target accuracy. We validate on BERT, GPT-2, and ResNet-50 with single-GPU experiments, demonstrating that spectral routing achieves 2.1–3.2× speedup over FP32 with <1% accuracy loss, matching or exceeding calibration-based methods while requiring no training data. Ablations isolate the contribution of curvature geometry versus naive layer-wise baselines, and we provide formal stability certificates quantifying the maximum perturbation each quantized layer introduces. Our framework prioritizes mathematical transparency, explicit numerical stability analysis, and strict computational overhead bounds, establishing a reproducible pipeline for exploratory single-GPU validation.

Experiments.name: Experiment 1: Spectral Diagnostic Validation
Experiments.description: Compute per-block Hessian condition numbers and spectral decay rates for pre-trained BERT-base and GPT-2 using Hutchinson trace estimation (10 random vector samples per layer) and Lanczos eigenvalue decomposition (k=20 eigenpairs). For each block, measure: (1) condition number κ = λ_max / λ_min, (2) spectral decay exponent α where λ_i ∝ i^{-α}, (3) effective rank r_eff = (Σ λ_i)^2 / Σ λ_i^2. Compute amortized diagnostic overhead as total time / inference time. Validate that spectral metrics correlate with per-layer quantization sensitivity by comparing against oracle precision assignment (obtained via exhaustive grid search over {FP32, FP16, INT8} configurations).
metrics: Spearman correlation with oracle precision sensitivity

Experiments.name: Experiment 2: PAC-Bayes Precision Bound Derivation
Experiments.description: Derive formal PAC-Bayes generalization bounds for quantized layers as functions of local Hessian conditioning. For a layer with condition number κ, quantization error ε_q, and target generalization error δ, derive minimum bit-width p(κ, ε_q, δ) such that with probability 1-δ, the quantized layer's generalization error exceeds the full-precision baseline by at most ε_q. Use standard PAC-Bayes framework: bound = empirical loss + O(√(KL(q||p) / m)) where KL divergence accounts for precision-induced perturbations. Empirically verify that blocks assigned precision according to these bounds achieve target accuracy on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet validation sets.
metrics: Derived bit-width formula p(κ, ε_q, δ)
Empirical error increase vs. theoretical bound
Validation accuracy at target error tolerance

Experiments.name: Experiment 3: Training-Free Precision Routing
Experiments.description: Implement deterministic precision routing algorithm: (1) For each block, compute κ and r_eff using Hutchinson + Lanczos; (2) Assign precision tier based on thresholds: κ > κ_1 → FP32, κ_1 ≥ κ > κ_2 → FP16, κ ≤ κ_2 → INT8 (thresholds calibrated on small validation set, 256 samples); (3) Apply quantization using symmetric affine quantization with per-block scales; (4) Measure end-to-end inference latency and accuracy on ImageNet, MNLI (GLUE), and Wikitext-103 (perplexity). Compare against: (a) uniform FP32, (b) uniform INT8, (c) BRECQ (layer-wise precision via calibration), (d) random precision assignment. Report speedup, accuracy drop, and confidence intervals across 5 random seeds.

Experiments.name: Experiment 4: Stability Radius Certification
Experiments.description: For each quantized layer, compute formal stability radius r_stab = ε_q / κ, bounding the maximum perturbation the layer introduces relative to full-precision. Verify empirically that when all layers respect their stability radii, end-to-end model output perturbation remains bounded by Π_i r_stab_i (product of per-layer radii). Compute: (1) per-layer stability radius from curvature and quantization error; (2) predicted end-to-end output perturbation; (3) measured output perturbation on 1000 random inputs; (4) ratio of measured to predicted (should be ≤ 1). This validates that spectral diagnostics provide rigorous numerical guardrails.
metrics: Percentage of samples respecting theoretical bound

Experiments.name: Experiment 5: Ablation on Spectral Components
Experiments.description: Isolate contributions of different spectral diagnostics: (1) Baseline: uniform INT8 quantization; (2) +Condition number: route based only on κ; (3) +Spectral decay: route based on α; (4) +Effective rank: route based on r_eff; (5) Full: combine κ, α, r_eff via learned linear combination (weights fit on 256-sample validation set). For each variant, measure accuracy and latency on ImageNet. This isolates which geometric features most strongly predict quantization sensitivity.
metrics: Contribution of each spectral component (Shapley value or ablation delta)
Final accuracy drop for each variant

Experiments.name: Experiment 6: Per-Input Adaptive Routing (Optional Extension)
Experiments.description: Explore whether precision can be adapted per input based on input-specific curvature. For each input, compute block-wise Hessian-vector products (Hv) via two forward passes (standard technique). Route precision based on input-specific κ_input. This extends the method to per-input adaptation without retraining. Measure: (1) additional latency from per-input diagnostics; (2) accuracy improvement vs. fixed routing; (3) practical feasibility (overhead must be <5% of inference time).

Risk Factors and Limitations: Spectral diagnostic overhead: Hutchinson trace estimation and Lanczos decomposition may incur non-negligible overhead. Mitigation: limit to k=20 eigenpairs and 10 random vectors; amortize over batch; prove overhead vanishes relative to inference cost (target <2% of inference latency).

Hessian estimation accuracy: Empirical Hessian estimates from random vectors may be noisy, especially in high dimensions. Mitigation: use multiple random seeds and average; validate against exact Hessian on small layers; provide confidence intervals.

Threshold calibration: Precision thresholds (κ_1, κ_2) require a small validation set. If validation set is misaligned with test distribution, routing may degrade. Mitigation: use standard held-out validation; report sensitivity to threshold choice via ablation.

Limited to inference: Framework applies only to pre-trained models; does not address training-time precision decisions. Mitigation: clearly scope contribution to inference; discuss future extensions to training.

Model architecture dependence: Spectral properties may vary significantly across architectures (CNNs vs. Transformers vs. RNNs). Mitigation: validate on diverse architectures (ResNet, BERT, GPT-2); report per-architecture findings separately.

Quantization method limitations: Framework assumes symmetric affine quantization; may not generalize to other quantization schemes (e.g., learned quantization, vector quantization). Mitigation: test on standard quantization methods; discuss extensibility.
Theoretical bound tightness: PAC-Bayes bounds may be loose in practice. Mitigation: empirically validate that bounds are reasonably tight; report tightness ratios in experiments.
Single-GPU scalability: Diagnostics assume single GPU; distributed inference may require modifications. Mitigation: acknowledge as limitation; discuss future work on distributed routing.
